behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive errors.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error construction in a single module. No real I/O, subprocess, or external service is required. Mocking does not hide the core failure because the behavior is local parsing logic.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with invalid cron strings (`* * * 13 *`, `* * 32 * *`, malformed token sequences). Assert it returns/throws structured errors containing field-specific messages (e.g., invalid month/day/syntax position), not generic failure text.

behaviors.description: Next-run calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron, including last-day-of-month and nearest-weekday rules.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is calendar rule computation in the scheduling algorithm. This is deterministic function logic and can be validated at function/object boundary without wiring full engine infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Given a parsed schedule object and fixed start timestamps, assert next N outputs exactly match expected UTC/local timestamps for representative cases: monthly on last day, nearest weekday to the 1st/15th, leap vs non-leap February, and month boundary rollovers.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules firing within minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: Real failures commonly occur at module boundaries: run-time generator output normalization (ordering/timezone/precision) consumed by overlap detection. A pure unit of overlap math can miss wiring mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser + next-run generator + overlap detector together. Create two cron schedules that produce runs within and outside a 5-minute threshold. Assert detector reports exact conflicting window pairs and excludes non-conflicts, including boundary case exactly equal to 5 minutes.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling is correct across DST spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on interaction between scheduler logic and real timezone/DST rules from the datetime/tz module. Not OS/network/subprocess dependent if zone database is local, but higher than unit because boundary semantics are critical.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real IANA timezone (e.g., `America/New_York`), run engine scheduling around known DST transition dates. Verify spring-forward handling for nonexistent 2:30 AM (documented policy: skip or shift) and fall-back duplicated 2:30 AM (single vs two runs per policy), with exact expected timestamps and offsets.

behaviors.description: When computed next run is already in the past, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill).
behaviors.justification: Failure appears when policy evaluation, schedule computation, and engine state/time reference are wired together. Unit tests of policy alone can miss incorrect handoff of computed times or policy config application.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate engine with controllable clock and persisted last-run state. For identical missed intervals, run once with `skip` and once with `backfill` policy; assert enqueue/execution decisions, produced run instances, and updated next-run state are correct and policy-specific.
